metadata.title: The RtHon Baroness Stowell of Beeston MBE Chair of the Communications and Digital Committee House of
metadata.author: download (21)

chunks.text: The RtHon Baroness Stowell of Beeston MBE Chair of the Communications and Digital Committee House of Lords London SW1A 0PW Tel: 020 7219 2922 Email: holcommunications@parliament. uk The RtHon Michelle Donelan MP Secretary of State for Digital, Culture, Media and Sport 100 Parliament Street London SW1A 2BQ 20 December 2022 Dear Secretary of State, I write regarding the Government’s recent amendments to the Online Safety Bill. As you will be aware, the Communications and Digital Committee takes a keen interest in online safety and freedom of expression. We took evidence on 6 December on the Government’s new approach to the Online Safety Bill. We appreciate the Government faces calls from all sides to amend the Bill. Oneofthemost pressing issues we recommend you address is the integrity of the independent regulator Ofcom. The Bill is rightly structured to ensure that Ofcom sits at the heart of the en for cement regime. But that system will only command public confidence if Ofcom can maintain, and be seen to maintain, its independence. The powers granted to the Secretary of State by the Bill risk undermining this. We note that the Government has a range of tools for engaging with Ofcom in other are as of media regulation. But the Bill’s provisions are unnecessarily extensive. They go beyond setting strategic direction and scope, and include powers that could be used to interfere with the Ofcom’s implementation work. The broad power in Clause 39 to direct Ofcom to modify a draft code of practice on Ofcom’s duties “for reasons of public policy” is one example. Related provisions enable the Secretary of State to engage in repeated back-and-forth with Ofcom before the matter is subject to parliamentary oversight. Such powers could be used to impose the Government’s view on Ofcom before important issues reach Parliament. Lorna Woods, Professor of Internet Law at the University of Essex, told us it is “entirely right” for Parliament to set the strategic direction, “but how it is implemented is for the independent regulator. We have a number of provisions where the Government can interfere.” Dr Edina Harbinja of Aston University agreed that the “Secretary of State’s Questions 9 and 10 , oral evidence on the Online Safety Bill Online Safety Bill as amended on recommittal in Public Bill Committee dated 19 December. Previously this power wassetout in Clause 41, see Online Safety Bill as amended on Report dated 6 December 2022. See also Digital, Culture, Media and Sport Committee, Amending the Online Safety Bill (First Report, Session 2022–23) p 5 Question 9 , oral evidence on the Online Safety Bill powers are too broad and overarching”. Matthew Feeney, Head of Tech & Innovation at the Centre for Policy Studies, likewise argued these powers should be reviewed. The operational independence of the regulator is fundamental to the wider principle of a free broadcasting environment. The Government has recently taken significant decisions to amend the Bill’s scope.

chunks.text: overarching”. Matthew Feeney, Head of Tech & Innovation at the Centre for Policy Studies, likewise argued these powers should be reviewed. The operational independence of the regulator is fundamental to the wider principle of a free broadcasting environment. The Government has recently taken significant decisions to amend the Bill’s scope. We would be grateful for clarification as to why the Government did not take the opportunity to change course on the crucial issue of regulatory independence. We would also be grateful for clarification on the implications of amendments to the (former) Clause 12, which have removed obligations on tech firms to provide risk assessments and transparency. We note that these changes were not accompanied by detailed explanatory notes in the Government’s communications or Written Ministerial Statements. Carnegie UK has argued that “the requirement for risk assessment has moved from being explicit and public to implicit and private”. They suggest users maynothave the in for mation to assess the nature of risks on the platform or the efficacy and appropriateness ofthenewuser empowerment tools. We would be grateful for confirmation as to whether firms in scope will be required to undertake risk assessments and publish transparency reports in relation tothenewuser empowerment tools. We would appreciate a response by 13 January 2023. We look forward to discussing these issues further at our upcoming evidence session with the Parliamentary Under-Secretary of State for Tech and the Digital Economy, Paul Scully MP, and Lord Parkinson of Whitley Bay. Yours sincerely, BARONESS STOWELL OF BEESTON Ibid. Question 10 , oral evidence on the Online Safety Bill Carnegie UK, ‘Online Safety Bill – Government amendments for Committee stage’ (December 2022): Online Safety Bill – Government amendments for Committee stage - Carnegie UK Trust
chunks.display_text: We would be grateful for clarification as to why the Government did not take the opportunity to change course on the crucial issue of regulatory independence. We would also be grateful for clarification on the implications of amendments to the (former) Clause 12, which have removed obligations on tech firms to provide risk assessments and transparency. We note that these changes were not accompanied by detailed explanatory notes in the Government’s communications or Written Ministerial Statements. Carnegie UK has argued that “the requirement for risk assessment has moved from being explicit and public to implicit and private”. They suggest users maynothave the in for mation to assess the nature of risks on the platform or the efficacy and appropriateness ofthenewuser empowerment tools. We would be grateful for confirmation as to whether firms in scope will be required to undertake risk assessments and publish transparency reports in relation tothenewuser empowerment tools. We would appreciate a response by 13 January 2023. We look forward to discussing these issues further at our upcoming evidence session with the Parliamentary Under-Secretary of State for Tech and the Digital Economy, Paul Scully MP, and Lord Parkinson of Whitley Bay. Yours sincerely, BARONESS STOWELL OF BEESTON Ibid.
